Indigenous Australians Civil Rights Movement← All Lessons
Lesson 4 of 4 — Final Lesson

The Apology & Contemporary Issues

💭 Thinking Question

If someone had hurt you deeply — perhaps taken something precious from you — and many years later said "sorry," what would that mean to you? Would saying sorry be enough? What would need to happen after the apology for it to be truly meaningful?

🎯 Learning Intention

By the end of this lesson I will be able to analyse repetition and emotive language in the National Apology speech, evaluate whether the Apology has been followed by meaningful action, examine the 2023 Voice Referendum, and assess progress on Closing the Gap targets.

⚠️ Warning: This lesson contains historical references to harm done to First Nations peoples. Please engage with care and respect.

The National Apology to the Stolen Generations

Study the Apology introduction and context from your booklet:

📹 Watch
The National Apology — Prime Minister Kevin Rudd, 13 February 2008

⚠️ As you watch, note any words or phrases that are REPEATED, and any words that are particularly emotive.

The Apology Transcript

Read the full transcript from your booklet carefully before completing the language analysis questions:

The response to the Apology by Tom Calma, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Social Justice Commissioner:

The Apology — Repetition & Emotive Language

1Write down the words and phrases that are repeated during the speech. Then explain why you think Rudd chose to repeat them.
Repeated words/phrases I noticed:
Why he repeated them (effect on the audience):
2Identify at least three emotive words or phrases from the speech and explain the emotional response each creates:
Emotive word/phrase 1 + effect:
Emotive word/phrase 2 + effect:
Emotive word/phrase 3 + effect:
3Tom Calma said: "This is not about black armbands and guilt. It never was. It is about belonging." What do you think he means?
4How do you think people in the crowd felt when they first heard the words "we say sorry" spoken by a Prime Minister? Why was this significant?

Turning Words Into Actions

Study the Apology Reflection activity from your booklet:

The Apology Reflection — Turning Words Into Actions

The PM said: "We today take this first step by acknowledging the past and laying claim to a future that embraces all Australians." Think of three actions for each column below.

Me — Personal Actions
My School
The Government
Write a letter to the school Principal (Mr Stassinopoulos) outlining practical actions to acknowledge the past and embrace the future:

Dear Mr Stassinopoulos,

Contemporary Issues

Study the Closing the Gap and 2023 Referendum content from your booklet:

Closing the Gap Analysis

Study the Closing the Gap analysis task and infographics from your booklet — you need the data from these two pages to fill in the table below:

Closing the Gap Data Table

Using the infographics above, find the figures for both the Indigenous population and the Australian population for each outcome.

OutcomeIndigenous PopulationAustralian Population
Male Life Expectancy
Female Life Expectancy
Healthy Birth Weight
Enrolled in Pre-School
Adults in Prison (per 100,000)
Young People in Detention (per 10,000)
Children Out of Home Care (per 1,000)
People Who Took Own Life (per 100,000)
Students Developmentally on Track
Year 12 or Equivalent
Tertiary Qualification
15–24 Employed/Education
Adults Employed
Appropriate Sized Housing
1In which areas is the gap largest? What does this tell us about the long-term effects of the policies studied throughout this unit?
2The Apology was delivered in 2008 with a promise to close the gap. Looking at the data, how would you evaluate the progress made since then?
3The 2023 Voice Referendum was rejected by 60% of voters. Given what you've learned throughout this unit, what does this result mean for the relationship between Indigenous and non-Indigenous Australians? Consider different perspectives.

🪞 Final Lesson & Unit Reflection

This is your final reflection for the whole unit. Take time to think carefully.

The title of this unit is "Indigenous Australians Civil Rights Movement." Looking back at everything — from the White Australia Policy to Closing the Gap — has the civil rights movement succeeded?
What is the single most important thing this unit has taught you that you didn't know before? Why does it matter to you personally?
The Apology said "the injustices of the past must never, never happen again." What needs to happen — in Australia, in your community, and in your own life — to keep that promise?

🎉 You've Completed the Unit!

Thank you for engaging with the history of Indigenous Australians' Civil Rights Movement with care and respect. Download your answers as a Word document and submit them to your teacher.
